title: Baton Project Context
description: The adopting project's declared identity and stakes. Hand-authored, version-controlled, and read by the engine to fill the {{project_context}} and {{project_stakes}} substitutions in every LLM check's user payload (see baton/llm-checks/README.md). DECLARED, not inferred: an engine may fall back to detecting a project's technology from its files, but detection cannot know whether the system serves real customers or handles money — and that is precisely what should move a security finding from advisory to blocking. An engine that infers rather than reads this record must say so, because a guess presented to the model as a fact is graded against as a fact.

ratification.description: Human ratification of the artefact — the same elicited → ratified → durable contract Rule 10 applies to journeys, for the same reason. The model DRAFTS this record by reading the repo (it can see the stack, the auth code, the payment integrations, the schemas holding PII). It cannot know whether real customers depend on the system, and it must not decide that on the human's behalf. An unratified record is a PROPOSAL: an engine must treat its stakes as HIGH (fail closed) until a human has reviewed and ratified it.

ratified.description: True when a human has reviewed and ratified this record. False on a fresh model-drafted record.

drafted_by.description: The model that drafted this record, e.g. "openai/gpt-5.6". Recorded so a reader knows the draft's provenance — and so a stale draft from a weaker model can be re-elicited.

context.description: One line, in the voice of a sentence completing 'You are evaluating a slice in a release of ___'. State the stack as a reviewer would need it: languages, frameworks, and the data layer. Example: 'a Next.js and TypeScript frontend with a Go backend on Postgres'.

stakes.description: What is at risk if a defect ships. Engines MUST act on this: a project in production serving real users, or handling sensitive data, raises the blocking threshold of the security-review check (see baton/llm-checks/security-review.md) — at high stakes a `medium` finding blocks instead of advising. FAIL CLOSED: stakes that are absent, or present on an unratified record, are treated as HIGH, not low. A model may propose stakes from what it reads in the repo, but only a human can confirm that real people depend on the system, so an unconfirmed proposal must not be able to LOWER the bar. An undeclared system is not a safe one; it is an unexamined one.

production.description: The code is deployed and live, not a prototype or an internal tool.

real_users.description: Real people outside the team depend on this system.

sensitive_data.description: Categories of sensitive data the system holds or processes. A non-empty list means a leak has a real-world victim.

regulated.description: Free-text regime(s) the system is subject to, e.g. 'APRA CPS 234', 'PCI-DSS', 'GDPR'. Informational: it sharpens the model's priors, and an engine may surface it, but the blocking threshold is driven by production/real_users/sensitive_data.

notes.description: Anything else a reviewer would want to know before judging severity.

examples.context: a Next.js and TypeScript frontend with a Go backend on Neon Postgres

stakes.notes: A defect here reaches paying customers and their financial records.
